title: Training Independent Subnetworks for Robust Prediction (MIMO)
authors: Marton Havasi, Rodolphe Jenatton, Stanislav Fort, Jeremiah Zhe Liu, Jasper Snoek, Balaji Lakshminarayanan, Andrew M. Dai, Dustin Tran (University of Cambridge; Google Research; Harvard)
tokenization_method: MIMO proposes NO tokenizer. It is an architectural ensembling mechanism: take a standard network and make two minimal changes. (1) Input layer: concatenate M independently-sampled datapoints {x_1..x_M} before the first hidden layer. (2) Output layer: replace the single head with M heads producing M predictive distributions, head m trained to predict y_m for its matching input x_m only. Because features derived from the OTHER inputs are useless for predicting a given head's matching input, the heads learn to ignore them, and M independent subnetworks self-organize inside one shared network (verified by per-activation conditional-variance analysis: almost every pre-activation has non-zero variance w.r.t. exactly one input). At test time the single query x' is tiled M times (x_1=...=x_M=x') and the M head predictions are averaged into one ensemble prediction computed in a SINGLE forward pass.
feature_types_handled: Demonstrated only on image inputs (CNN feature maps on CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet). The mechanism is input-agnostic and operates on whatever the base network's input layer accepts; it does not itself distinguish numerical / categorical / temporal / missing tabular feature types. Its tabular descendant TabM handles mixed tabular features, but MIMO as published does not.
token_granularity: Not a token-level method. The unit of multiplexing is a whole datapoint (M whole inputs concatenated), not per-feature tokens. Orthogonal to token granularity.
numerical_strategy: n/a (no feature encoding contribution; inputs are raw image tensors in the paper).
scales_to_many_features: Does NOT address many-feature scaling. MIMO's scaling axis is the number of ensemble members M (1-5 studied; M=2-4 optimal), not the number of input features. Cost: M=3 adds only ~0.03% parameters and ~0.01% FLOPs vs the base ResNet, and prediction wall-clock is ~equal to a single deterministic net (e.g. 0.639ms vs 0.632ms/example on TPUv2) while Deep Ensemble (M=4) costs ~4x (2.536ms). So it is a near-free robustness/efficiency wrapper, NOT a feature-scaling technique.
downstream_model: Convolutional networks: Wide ResNet-28-10 (CIFAR), ResNet-50 (ImageNet), plus a SmallCNN for analysis. Consumes raw inputs; no transformer/tokenizer involved. (The idea was later ported onto tabular MLPs by TabM.)
pretraining_objective: none. Standard supervised training; loss is the sum of the M per-head negative log-likelihoods over the M independently-sampled training inputs.
key_results: Best single-forward-pass method on all three benchmarks. CIFAR-10/ResNet28-10 (M=3): Acc 96.4 / NLL 0.123 / ECE 0.010 vs Deterministic 96.0 / 0.159 / 0.023 and BatchEnsemble(M=4) 96.2 / 0.143 / 0.018; approaches Deep Ensemble(M=4) 96.6 / 0.115 / 0.008 at ~1/4 the prediction time. CIFAR-100 (M=3): Acc 82.0 / NLL 0.690 / ECE 0.022 vs Deterministic 79.8 / 0.875 / 0.086. ImageNet/ResNet50 (M=2, rho=0.6): Acc 77.5 / NLL 0.887 vs Deterministic 76.1 / 0.943; Wide-MIMO 79.3. Gains hold on corrupted/OOD variants (cAcc, cNLL, cECE). Member diversity (disagreement) matches that of independently trained networks, far above Naive-multihead/TreeNet/BatchEnsemble.
baselines_compared: Deterministic SGD net, MC-Dropout, Naive multihead, TreeNet, BatchEnsemble (M=4), Thin Ensemble (M=4), Deep Ensemble (M=4); via Google Uncertainty Baselines for well-tuned references.
transferable_idea: Multiplex M independent ensemble members through ONE shared backbone via multi-input/multi-output heads, getting Deep-Ensemble-level diversity, calibration, and OOD robustness at a single forward pass and ~0.03% extra params. For this topic it is the conceptual seed of TabM-style tabular ensembling: wrap any feature tokenizer + downstream model in a MIMO head to harden 70+-feature predictions cheaply. The input-repetition coefficient rho (share x_1=x_2 with prob rho) is the key knob that makes MIMO work when the backbone has limited excess capacity.
limitations: MIMO needs a backbone with EXCESS capacity to host M diverse subnetworks; when capacity is tight (ResNet50/ImageNet at M=2) independent inputs hurt and you must raise rho (used 0.6) which trades away diversity. Optimal M is small (2-4) and is an extra tuned hyperparameter; batch-repetition (4x CIFAR, 2x ImageNet) raises training cost to ~match BatchEnsemble. Evaluated only on image CNNs in this paper; no tabular, sequence, or transformer evidence. Accuracy peaks at smaller M than log-likelihood, so the best M depends on the target metric.
pitfalls: (1) MIMO is frequently mis-filed as a tokenization or feature-interaction method; it is neither — it contributes zero to how features are represented and is orthogonal to / composes with any tokenizer. (2) 'For free' refers to PREDICTION compute only; training cost rises with batch repetition, so it is not free to train. (3) Benefit is contingent on backbone over-parameterization — a tightly-fit tabular MLP may show little gain at rho=0 (this is exactly why TabM had to adapt the recipe rather than drop MIMO in unchanged). (4) Member independence (the whole point) collapses as rho approaches 1, so rho must be tuned per backbone.
relevance_to_topic: Indirect but real: MIMO is the ancestor of TabM's parameter-efficient tabular ensembling. It does not tokenize 70+ heterogeneous features, but it is a near-zero-cost robustness/calibration wrapper that composes with whatever tokenizer feeds a deep tabular model.
